It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Assessing the institutionalization of private sustainability governance in a changing coffee sector Janina Grabs Institute of Political Science, Westfälische Wilhelms-Universität Münster, Münster, Germany Abstract The potential of transnational private governance initiatives to constitute effective alternatives to state-led regulation of global value chains rests on their ability to scale up and become institutionalized in a given sector. This study examines whether such institu- tionalization has occurred in the coffee sector, the commodity with the most widespread adoption of certiﬁed products and over 30 years’ experience of private governance, and tests hypotheses on facilitating and inhibiting conditions. It ﬁnds that while norm generation around responsible supply chain management and the organizational institutionalization of standard-setting bodies is well advanced, the practice of internalizing social and environmental externalities through the routinized production and purchase of higher priced certiﬁed goods continues to be questioned by industry actors. Indeed, conditions that favored normative and orga- nizational institutionalization, such as high levels of industry concentration, product differentiation, and deliberative interaction, are shown to represent barriers to the practice-oriented institutionalization of market-driven regulatory governance. Keywords: certiﬁcation, corporate social responsibility, environmental governance, private governance, sustainability. 1. Introduction Transnational private governance initiatives have emerged as promising tools to tackle the increasingly global economic and political challenges of our time (Cutler et al. 1999; Mattli & Woods 2009a; Newell & Paterson 2010; Büthe & Mattli 2011; Green 2013). Recent years have seen a particular proliferation of private initiatives attempting to govern the sustainability of agricultural production chains (cf. Fransen 2015; Fransen et al. 2016), driven by two core concerns: First, despite decades of efforts by state and non-state actors alike, the production of many agricultural commodities continues to be deﬁned by smallholder poverty (DeFries et al. 2017). Second, the adoption of sustainable production practices is key for ecosystem and biodiversity protection, as well as assur- ing long-term supply availability. In the absence of coordinated state action, externally veriﬁed certiﬁcation sys- tems such as Fairtrade, Rainforest Alliance, and UTZ Certiﬁed were established to address these challenges (Auld 2014; Lernoud et al. 2017). When considering these certiﬁcation systems as representations of private sustainability governance, we can dif- ferentiate three approaches that focus on norms, organizations, and practices, respectively. Under the most expan- sive, normative interpretation, certiﬁcation systems are examples of a broader set of private governance tools that allow downstream value chain actors (i.e. intermediate buyers and ﬁnal consumers) to take on a certain social or environmental responsibility and effectively set and enforce rules for upstream production practices (Scherer & Palazzo 2007; Auld et al. 2008; Scherer et al. 2016). From an organizational perspective, one can view standard- setting bodies (and possible meta-governance organizations) as creating private authority over production and trade rules vested in the organization (and, more broadly, the organizational ﬁeld) itself (Bartley 2007a; Fransen 2018). Finally, from a practice-oriented perspective, certiﬁcation systems embody a speciﬁc type of private sustainability governance that is deﬁned by the private setting of regulative rules in the production process that go above and beyond the legal status quo (Pattberg 2005a; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Vogel 2008). Such private regulations are rolled out through market-driven incentive mechanisms in which access to differentiated markets and price pre- miums is only made available to producers that follow the speciﬁed rules (Cashore et al. 2004; Bernstein & Cashore Correspondence: Janina Grabs, Institute of Political Science, Westfälische Wilhelms-Universität Münster, Scharnhorststrasse 100, 48151 Münster, Germany. Email: janina.grabs@uni-muenster.de Accepted for publication 12 July 2018. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Regulation & Governance (2018) doi:10.1111/rego.12212 Page 2: 2007). This type of private sustainability governance – which we can denominate as market-driven regulatory gov- ernance – thus represents a speciﬁc set of collective practices of both participating producers and buyers. The promise of certiﬁcation schemes to provide governance in global arenas where state actors were increas- ingly powerless created much interest in their potential to become legitimate alternatives to the state (Cashore et al. 2004). In response, a number of authors have provided cautiously optimistic (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Scherer & Palazzo 2007; Mattli & Woods 2009b), skeptical (Auld et al. 2007; Bartley 2007b; Abbott & Snidal 2009; Fransen 2011), or highly critical (Higgins & Lawrence 2005; Raynolds 2009; Graz & Nölke 2012) argu- ments on the likelihood and preconditions for their institutionalization to occur within global markets. This study sets out to test the resulting hypotheses in a comparative manner using empirical evidence from the coffee sector. To do so, we must ﬁrst understand what the institutionalization of private sustainability governance through certiﬁcation systems means in this context. The deﬁnition strongly depends on the interpretation of private sus- tainability governance described above. From the normative perspective, we may consider that institutionaliza- tion commences as soon as downstream market actors share a wide normative agreement that it is their responsibility to ensure products that enter their supply chain fulﬁll speciﬁc socio-economic and environmental requirements, and incorporate these norms into their standard operating procedures by pursuing a range of strategies to that effect (Risse & Sikkink 1999). From the organizational perspective, institutionalization may re- present the infusion of certiﬁcation organizations with value “beyond the technical requirement of the task at hand” (Selznick 1957, p. 17), allowing them to shift their programmatic or ideological goals because they are valued as organizations per se (Selznick 1957; Huntington 1968; Levitsky 1998). Finally, from the practice- oriented perspective, the institutionalization of market-driven regulatory governance through certiﬁcation sys- tems would represent the broad acceptance and implementation by both supply and demand-side actors of trade practices that effectively internalize social and environmental externalities through the routinized production and purchase of higher priced certiﬁed goods (North 1990; Jepperson 1991; O’Donnell 1994 1996; Levitsky 1998). This paper will focus on theoretical arguments and practical evidence related to the last perspective – both because it most closely reﬂects the theoretical and practical concern over certiﬁcation “upscaling” and “mainstreaming” (Raynolds 2009; Dolan 2010; Kolk 2013; Panhuysen & Pierrot 2014, 2018; Lernoud et al. 2017; Solér et al. 2017), and because it goes to the heart of the problem-solving effectiveness of private sustainability schemes, as the results will show.1 However, as will become apparent in the discussion, the different types of institutionalization of private sustainability governance may coexist and indeed inﬂuence each other in intricate ways. Furthermore, I primarily focus here on whether institutionalization occurs at the global level of industry, as the institutionalization of private sustainability governance in niche markets alone suffers from two drawbacks: First, it is continuously confronted with mainstream market forces, which may lead to regulatory dilution and backsliding (Mattli & Woods 2009b; Raynolds 2009; Dolan 2010; Kolk 2013). Second, it is unlikely to affect meaningful socio-economic and environmental change at a scale concomitant with its mission.2 The coffee indus- try, where private sustainability certiﬁcation and veriﬁcation schemes have existed for over 30 years and cover almost half of the world’s production volume, provides a powerful case study to explore whether, how, and why institutionalization at the global level may (not) be occurring, and to test theoretical assumptions to that effect. The coffee sector inhabits a ﬂagship position in responsible value chain organization. It is one of the largest industries that connects producers from the Global South with consumers in the North and has historically led efforts to make supply chains more sustainable (Gresser & Tickell 2002; Bacon 2005; Kolk 2005; Levy et al. 2016; Lernoud et al. 2017). The concept of what today is known as sustainably certiﬁed coffee emerged in the 1980s with the ﬁrst Fair Trade initiatives and gained prominence in the 2000s, leading to the emergence of numerous certiﬁca- tion schemes as important market actors (Giovannucci & Ponte 2005; Manning et al. 2012; Auld 2014; Potts et al. 2014). More recently, the concept of directly traded and single-origin agricultural products emerged ﬁrst in coffee (Wilson & Wilson 2014) before being replicated in cocoa and other crops. Trends within sustainability gov- ernance in coffee may thus be indicative of broader changes in the global landscape of sustainability initiatives. The study is based on an in-depth analysis of recent literature, documents, and data related to the coffee sec- tor, as well as over 60 expert interviews and observations gathered from ﬁeldwork in Costa Rica, Colombia, Hon- duras, and Guatemala and the attendance of various industry events between 2015 and 2016. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 J. Grabs Institutionalizing private governance Page 3: The analysis ﬁnds that the industry-wide engagement of market actors within the coffee sector has contrib- uted to an emerging logic of appropriateness in business practices and a normative institutionalization of the importance of sustainable sourcing practices. However, counter to Bernstein and Cashore’s (2007) legitimacy- building framework, this normative alignment has not occurred regarding the means to achieve sustainable sourc- ing; rather more business-friendly alternatives to market-driven regulatory governance (such as internal traceabil- ity systems) are beginning to emerge. This development can best be explained by actor-based theories underpinned by a utilitarian logic of consequences. As predicted by Auld et al. (2007) and Vogel (2009), an insuf- ﬁcient increase in demand caused oversupply of certiﬁed coffee, reducing its economic beneﬁt to producers. Fit- ting the political–institutional perspective of Bartley (2007b) and Fransen (2011), the contentious, underlying distributional problems present in the coffee sector constituted a barrier to the institutionalization of market- driven regulatory governance. Finally, critical, system-based theories – such as neo-Gramscian views of power dynamics in buyer-driven value chains (Graz & Nölke 2012; Levy et al. 2016) – provide explanatory power for buyers’ motivations to use certiﬁcation schemes, but do not adequately theorize the ability of producing country representatives to respond with their own interpretations of sustainability, as well as the steady rise of direct impact projects as alternatives to certiﬁcation. In its discussion, the paper highlights that the normative or organi- zational institutionalization of private sustainability governance may stem from preconditions that in turn repre- sent barriers to the practice-based institutionalization of market-driven regulatory governance, and derives implications for future research. 2. Theoretical perspectives on the institutionalization of market-driven regulatory governance practices The prospects and necessary conditions for externally veriﬁed certiﬁcation schemes – alternatively conceptual- ized as non-state market-driven (NSMD) governance systems (Cashore 2002; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Auld et al. 2009), private governance organizations (Fransen 2011), regulatory standard-setting schemes (Abbott & Snidal 2009), transnational private regulation (Bartley 2007b), and deﬁned here as market-driven regulatory governance mechanisms – to emerge as broadly accepted institutions that effectively address social and envi- ronmental problems are widely debated in a number of overlapping disciplines. How optimistic authors are regarding the widespread adoption, legitimacy, and effectiveness of such schemes is closely related to their view of ﬁrms’ core motivations, as well as schemes’ capacity to either change these motivations or align with them while preserving their core mission. We can sort approaches and arguments into two broad camps: those that argue that the evaluation and, consequently, the evolution of schemes underlies a utilitarian logic of conse- quences; and those that allow that a logic of appropriateness, deﬁned as a situation where actors make “the commitment to fulﬁll an identity without regard to its consequences for personal or group preferences or inter- ests” (March & Olsen 1996, p. 254), may increasingly apply (Bernstein & Cashore 2007). I start by reviewing the latter somewhat more optimistic perspective before turning to three counterarguments made by supporters of a logic of consequences based primarily on economic, political, or power considerations. This overview fur- ther allows us to derive four conditions (moving from most to least likely) for the institutionalization of market-driven regulatory sustainability governance that will be empirically examined in Section 4. 2.1. A logic of appropriateness may increasingly replace a logic of consequences The main proponents of this approach are Bernstein and Cashore (2007) with their theory of how NSMD gover- nance may achieve political legitimacy. In their view, after the emergence of such schemes and a period of strate- gic struggle guided mainly by the logic of consequences, learning processes may lead to the emergence of shared norms that explain the convergence of standards on key criteria, as well as a “re-deﬁnition of disparate interests and the prerequisites for widespread community building” (Bernstein & Cashore 2007, p. 358; cf. Scherer & Palazzo 2007; Mattli & Woods 2009b). Such normative pressure could then lead to the disappearance of the most fundamental conﬂicts and the emergence of a shared political community in which NSMD systems – that is, market-driven regulatory gover- nance mechanisms – are considered the legitimate and appropriate arena for supply chain governance. At this © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Institutionalizing private governance J. Grabs Page 4: stage, strategic calculations are no longer made about participation in NSMD initiatives, but rather about shaping the processes and debates within the NSMD system itself (Bernstein & Cashore 2007). The analysis builds on Cashore’s (2002) legitimacy-building framework, in which he argues that for institutionalized NSMD systems to emerge, they must go beyond deriving their rulemaking authority from interest-based pragmatic legitimacy and value-oriented moral legitimacy. Instead the more durable, culturally-focused, cognitive legitimacy (Suchman 1995), based on “a cognitive evaluation that something is ‘understandable’ or ‘to do otherwise is unthinkable,’” must increasingly apply (Cashore 2002, p. 515). However, institutionalization is not a foregone conclusion for Bernstein and Cashore; indeed, they point out: [I]nstitutionalization may continue to be elusive […] owing to resistance to learning, mistrust, or deep divisions within the NSMD community, whether between ﬁrms and NGOs, among NGOs, or because other relevant actors such as indigenous communities may disagree on what governance norms are appropriate. (2007, p. 361) For Bernstein and Cashore, key conditions for the process of institutionalization to occur are therefore the creation of forums that allow for the exchange of expert information, the development of best practices, and a learning environment that allows stakeholders to build community and is based on broader global legitimating norms, such as fairness and deliberative processes (Bernstein & Cashore 2007, p. 363). We can hence state the ﬁrst hypothesis for institutionalization as follows: Hypothesis 1: The more market actors participate in intrasectoral engagement within best practice forums and learning envi- ronments, the greater the likelihood that shared new ideas and norms of appropriate business behavior will be generated and diffused that help establish a political community of practice and the institutionalization of market-driven regulatory governance (Bernstein & Cashore 2007). 2.2. A logic of consequences continues to dominate On the other hand, a number of authors are skeptical that ﬁrms can be swayed from their proﬁt-maximizing motives. Under those circumstances, economic, political, or power dynamics are seen to drive forward – or impede – institutionalization. 2.2.1. Economic perspectives Abbott and Snidal (2009) view business behavior primarily through a strategic lens, where corporate social responsibility (CSR) is treated as a competitive asset, reputational tool, and risk management strategy to avoid being targeted by shaming campaigns. Guided by their competitive environment, ﬁrms will thus seek ways to minimize compliance costs and pursue minimally intrusive ways of fulﬁlling stakeholder demands such as self- regulation. However, the authors predict that if industry associations or key ﬁrms manage to develop sector-wide rules that would raise costs equally for all competitors, ﬁrms will be more likely to buy into schemes (Abbott & Snidal 2009). This resonates with conceptualizations of market-driven regulatory governance schemes as rational solutions to an industry’s reputational collective action problems (Spar 1998; Potoski & Prakash 2005; cf. Pattberg 2005b). Taking a similar rationalist-economic viewpoint, Vogel (2009) asserts that successful upscaling of externally veriﬁed certiﬁcation schemes depends on sufﬁcient and consistent economic and political demand signals to pro- vide a continuous business case for engagement. However, the complexity of frequently competing sustainability goals and standards constitutes a “wicked problem” that may overwhelm institutional and individual consumers (Kolk 2013). According to Vogel (2009), this overreliance on market incentives limits the likely success of civil regulations to challenge the regulatory status quo. Auld et al. (2007) add that these limits may be self-reinforcing as non-governmental organizations (NGOs) make the strategic decision to focus on shaming campaigns rather than the stimulation of ﬁnal consumer demand when recruiting companies to certiﬁcation schemes, leading to short-term win-win scenarios but few changes in the fundamental market structure. From this rationalist– economic point of view, the institutionalization of the use of certiﬁcation schemes is thus possible, but only if market conditions are amenable to such developments: © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 J. Grabs Institutionalizing private governance Page 5: Hypothesis 2: The more sustained consumer demand exists, which provides a continued economic rationale for ﬁrms to par- ticipate in certiﬁcation schemes, the greater the likelihood that the institutionalization of market-driven regula- tory governance will occur (Auld et al. 2007; Vogel 2009). 2.2.2. Political perspectives Bartley (2007b) and Fransen (2011) add a political–institutional perspective which, while seeing ﬁrms as political actors in the vein of political CSR (Scherer & Palazzo 2007), conceptualizes transnational private governance as fundamentally political processes where certiﬁcation schemes arise as “by-product of conﬂicts over distributional gains” in international value chains (Bartley 2007b, p. 299). Focusing on certiﬁcation emergence, Bartley is skepti- cal that voluntary schemes can have signiﬁcant impacts on such distributional problems, but recognizes that “imperfect systems can sometimes create new points of leverage” (Bartley 2007b, p. 300) if civil society advocates can forge strong coalitions. Tracing the continued evolution of the private governance of such contentious issues, Fransen (2011) sees little likelihood of convergence toward effective sector-wide governance. Instead, deliberative stakeholder engagement may actually reinforce and entrench political differences through “negative trust spirals; increased commitment to own identities as being opposed to the other, heightened differences; and possible con- ﬂict” (Fransen 2011, p. 364). Nevertheless, Fransen (2011) identiﬁes a number of conditions that might avoid the contentious politicization of private governance: on an industry level, geographic and market homogeneity and simple competitive relations might provide a better basis for the convergence of interests; for civil society organizations, organizational and geographic homogeneity and high issue agreement might allow them to create stronger coalitions (cf. Bartley 2007a,b). Issue areas with low distributional consequences to the governance setup,3 a high ability to objectify the governance issue, and a limited historical legacy of conﬂict between actors might further help to decrease rather than increase contentious politics between interest groups and lead to effective governance systems. However, the more politicized the issue, the less likely it is that convergence toward strong private governance institutionaliza- tion will occur (Fransen 2011): Hypothesis 3: The lower the levels of contentious politicization of the governed sector or issue area, the greater the likelihood that institutionalization of market-driven regulatory governance will occur (Bartley 2007b; Fransen 2011). 2.2.3. Power perspectives Global political economy scholars share this pessimistic assumption. However, in contrast to the more meso-level, actor-centric approaches sketched out in parts 2.2.1 and 2.2.2, this discipline takes a system-centric view of the production chain and the use of private governance therein. Many authors in this tradition adopt neo-Gramscian perspectives when conceptualizing the difﬁculty of civic movements to successfully challenge the “hegemony” of neoliberal capitalist market structures dominated by powerful multinational corporations. They see a strong dan- ger that private governance schemes are instrumentalized as tools to reinforce unequal power dynamics.4 Explic- itly or implicitly, this perspective is particularly dominant in analyses of the governance of agrifood markets, including coffee (cf. Higgins & Lawrence 2005; Clapp & Fuchs 2009; Curran et al. 2009; Elder et al. 2014). In this view, corporate actors are likely to dominate the bargaining process and emerge victorious, using their “discursive power” (Fuchs et al. 2009) to strategically re-align the object of private regulation according to their own aims (e.g. Raynolds 2009). The only option left to less powerful challenger organizations is to nudge hege- mons toward a Gramscian “passive revolution” in which “a hegemonic system adapts and evolves as it absorbs challenges and preserves essential features” (Levy et al. 2016, p. 366); however, it remains questionable whether the ﬁnal result is will be in line with the movement’s original aims (cf. Guthman 2007; Raynolds et al. 2007). This preoccupation is particularly strong with regard to the mainstreaming of Fairtrade, which is analyzed critically as the “commodiﬁcation of morality” by some scholars (Raynolds 2009; Bacon 2010; Dolan 2010; Robbins 2013). This perspective thus leaves little room for the successful institutionalization of market-driven regulatory gover- nance in the current neoliberal market environment: © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Institutionalizing private governance J. Grabs Page 6: Hypothesis 4: The greater the presence of hegemonic power structures, the less likely it is that the institutionali- zation of market-driven regulatory governance will occur while maintaining the original mission of civic organi- zations (Raynolds et al. 2007; Graz & Nölke 2012; Levy et al. 2016). In reviewing the literature on the prospects and likely preconditions of the institutionalization of main- streamed but effective market-driven regulatory governance mechanisms, I have thus identiﬁed four main hypotheses that may play out within the governed markets:5 H1 proposes that deliberative processes, learning, and norm generation may drive institutionalization (Bernstein & Cashore 2007); H2 cautions that institutionali- zation will be difﬁcult without sustained economic demand drivers (Auld et al. 2007; Vogel 2009); H3 warns that fragmented sectors and civil society actors, as well as problems related to distributional imbalances, may impede institutionalization (Bartley 2007b, Fransen 2011); and H4 asserts that any type of meaningful institutionalization is unlikely to succeed within neoliberal markets with hegemonic power structures (Raynolds et al. 2007; Graz & Nölke 2012; Levy et al. 2016). With the exception of H4, these hypotheses are not mutually exclusive. H2 and H3 are in closest theoretical alignment, while Bernstein and Cashore (2007) also agree that a logic of consequences dominates actors’ choices when private governance initiatives ﬁrst emerge. Whether alternative norms that succeed in changing the “ratio- nal terms of exchange” (March & Olsen 1996, p. 250) are able to emerge then depends on the level, intensity, and quality of sectoral engagement; if such interactions do not materialize, the authors predict a stagnation of pri- vate governance initiatives in an intermediate phase with substantially misaligned objectives of different market and non-market actors. Many of these analyses are forward-looking because they were written at a time when it was too early to empirically examine the likely emergence of institutionalization processes. A decade later, the International Trade Centre (ITC) recognizes that “voluntary sustainability standards are in the mainstream, and no longer a novelty for niche markets” (Lernoud et al., 2017, p. i). It thus seems like an appropriate time to evaluate whether these mainstreaming activities have simultaneously led to a process of institutionalization and which of the four hypotheses may contribute toward explaining recent developments. This analysis will focus on the coffee sector, the most advanced crop that is “poised to become the ﬁrst sustainable commodity,” according to the ITC (Lernoud et al. 2017, p. 2). After summarizing the current status of certiﬁcation as a private sustainability gover- nance mechanism in coffee, it will examine the existence of facilitating or inhibiting conditions for the emergence of institutionalized private governance of each hypothesis in turn. The next section introduces the methods used in more detail. 3. Methods In order to provide a comprehensive analysis of recent developments in sustainability governance of the coffee sector, this study combines a variety of sources. First, I conducted a multidisciplinary literature review that spanned the disciplines of political science, politi- cal economy, development economics, management, and sociology in order to construct an accurate overview of the most up-to-date reviews of the coffee value chain and its sustainability. I furthermore collected secondary data on the market shares and inﬂuence of various actors of the supply chain, relying mainly on published statistics and acquisition and merger announcements in order to identify the current lead ﬁrms. I then engaged in primary document analysis of companies’ websites, statements, and published interviews to identify their commitments and stance regarding sustainability initiatives. This secondary data was combined with primary data stemming from expert interviews as well as participant observation during ﬁeldwork in Latin America (Costa Rica, Colombia, Guatemala, and Honduras) between 2015 and 2016. I conducted over 60 semi-structured interviews with coffee producers, cooperative managers, exporters, traders, roasters, and café owners, 53 of which were audio recorded and 10 of which were recorded by hand. The distribution of the different value chain actors is listed in Table 1. All participants were assured anonymity in order to increase conﬁdence and the veracity of their statements. Furthermore, I attended nine coffee industry events at various levels of organization: the Semana Internacional de Café, a meeting point for large-scale coffee stakeholders, roasters, and traders, in 2015 and 2016; the 2016 Forum © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 6 J. Grabs Institutionalizing private governance Page 7: of the Specialty Coffee Association of America, a gathering of specialty coffee actors; the ﬁrst General Assembly of the Global Coffee Platform, an incipient platform aiming to become the central unifying force for large-scale sustainability efforts; the ﬁrst World Coffee Producers’ Forum, organized in 2017 in Medellin, Colombia; three producer forums in Costa Rica and Honduras focused on coffee quality and sustainability; and a local consulta- tion regarding the new Rainforest Alliance standard in Honduras. These events provided an in-depth setting to observe the intra-industry conversation regarding sustainability and many opportunities for additional informal dialogue with industry members. They were embedded in a larger data collection effort on ﬁeld-level impacts of sustainability certiﬁcations in Colombia, Honduras, and Costa Rica, in the process of which I was able to con- verse with a signiﬁcant number of coffee farmers on their views of the industry and its development and to trian- gulate the analytical conclusions presented through continuous feedback processes. 4. Results 4.1. The coffee certiﬁcation paradox: Widely used, but more widely contested From a budgetary perspective, to date governance through certiﬁcation and veriﬁcation schemes dominates the sustainability picture in the coffee sector: around 60 percent (US$190 million) of the coffee industry’s sustainabil- ity budget is spent on certiﬁcation and veriﬁcation premiums (Steemers 2016). As we can see in Table 1, in 2015 between 29 percent and 53 percent6 of global coffee supply was certiﬁed under a private sustainability standard, while purchases of certiﬁed coffee accounted for 23 percent of worldwide coffee exports (Pierrot 2016). This makes the coffee sector the industry where certiﬁcation has crossed furthest into the mainstream (Lernoud et al. 2017). At ﬁrst glance, this should provide an optimistic picture of the relevance and acceptance of certiﬁca- tion systems in this sector, but a number of caveats apply. First, we can see in Table 2, as well as Figure 1, that the vast share of “sustainable” coffee and the main increase in coffee volume over the last ﬁve years, both produced and sold, came from Common Code for the Cof- fee Community (4C) veriﬁcation. This “Common Code for the Coffee Community” initially aimed to be an entry-level standard that prepared small producers for a quick progression toward other coffee certiﬁcations. However, its status as a baseline, meta- governance initiative was put into question by its ascendency to ISEAL (at the same level as Fairtrade Interna- tional, Rainforest Alliance, and UTZ Certiﬁed) and the use of 4C-veriﬁed coffee by roasters as evidence of their Table 1 Overview of expert interviews Interviewee type Traders Roasters Producer organizations Institutions NGOs Fellow researchers Number of interviews 18 13 10 5 10 6 NGOs, non-governmental organizations. Grabs Page 8: scaling up of sustainability standards in their supply chains (Fransen 2015; Millard 2017). In recent years, large roasting companies have abandoned the original stepping stone approach, arguing that the 4C code is sufﬁcient to guarantee sustainability in the value chain.7 Most recently, in 2016, the 4C Association, the multi-stakeholder initiative that originated the 4C standard, transformed itself into the Global Coffee Platform, spun off the 4C standard, and tasked a newly established for-proﬁt entity called “Coffee Assurance Services” with its management and further development, further blurring the lines between 4C and any other externally veriﬁed standards in either use or organizational set-up. However, it continues to show strong substantive differences with other third-party certiﬁcations. 4C adherence is primarily guaranteed through self-evaluation, with external veriﬁcation only occurring every three years and in a small sample of producers. Its code furthermore only includes a limited number of non- negotiable requirements, some of which lie below national law, leading the Institute for Multi-stakeholder Initiative Integrity to conclude that “4C’s standards do not impose stringent enough obligations to resolve the human rights concerns they seek to address” (MSI Integrity 2013, p. 5). Impact evaluations have equally found it to be relatively ineffective in producing on-farm sustainability improvements; in particular, environ- mental outcomes were unaffected even after eight years of program participation (Kuit et al. 2010, 2016).8 Finally, according to Coffee Assurance Services, only 41 percent of veriﬁed groups apply for re-veriﬁcation after three years (personal communication),9 leading to limited continuity and learning opportunities.10 While other standards, such as UTZ Certiﬁed, Rainforest Alliance, or the popular combination of Fairtrade and organic, contain stricter requirements and place greater demands on the producers that are subject to them, their effectiveness in creating socio-economic and environmental improvements on the ground has also increas- ingly been questioned.11 In addition, their growth rates have been much more moderate, with demand for the four certiﬁcation schemes combined rising from 7 percent to 10 percent of global exports from 2010 to 2015 (Global Coffee Platform [GCP] 2015; International Coffee Organization [ICO] 2016a). We can thus in a ﬁrst step conclude that coffee still seems far from becoming “the ﬁrst sustainable commodity” (Lernoud et al. 2017, p. 2), even if sourcing patterns have shown promising trends to date. Furthermore, at present industry actors increasingly question whether externally veriﬁed certiﬁcation schemes are adequate and cost-effective avenues to reach their goals. This is particularly pronounced in the language of two new industry platforms that emerged almost simultaneously in 2015 and 2016: The Global Coffee Platform (GCP) and the Sustainability Coffee Challenge (SCC).12 Figure 1 Coffee production and sales of major certiﬁcation schemes. However, […] the time has come for the coffee sector to […] take a more col- laborative, holistic and aligned approach to sustainability, aligning the different programs and initiatives towards a uniﬁed vision and progress framework. (GCP 2016a) Equally, the SCC turned its focus away from certiﬁcation and veriﬁcation standards, instead working “to bet- ter understand the non-certiﬁcation commitments that are being made by the sector and how these contribute to the transformation of the coffee sector” (Conservation International 2016a). Simultaneously, the largest coffee roasters have lowered their emphasis on the use of certiﬁcations in their sus- tainable sourcing strategies. As of early 2018, Jacobs Douwe Egberts (JDE) had not published any concrete commit- ments toward sustainable coffee sourcing for the future (Panhuysen & Pierrot 2018), while Nestlé announced that it would review its commitment to source 90,000 tons of standard-compliant coffee “to re-align it towards a rein- forced emphasis on enabling positive impacts on coffee farmers, their communities and landscapes” (Nestle 2017). Keurig Green Mountain’s 2020 target is to source 100 percent of its primary products “according to established Keurig Green Mountain Responsible Sourcing Guidelines” (Keurig Green Mountain 2017), in which certiﬁcations are included as one option among many others, including a company-internal traceability program. Producing country actors are similarly searching for new avenues to improve sustainability outside of tradi- tional certiﬁcation channels: The National Federation of Coffee Growers of Colombia (FNC), taking the lead on this issue, has repeatedly asserted in frustration that “standards have focused intensively on environmental sus- tainability, but [have] left economic sustainability issues behind” (Velez 2015). The FNC thus called for a “Global Economic Accord” based on farmer proﬁtability (Brown 2015), culminating in the ﬁrst World Coffee Producers’ Forum in July 2017. The Producers’ Forum was dominated by concerns over the falling real prices of coffee, and the Forum’s closing statement called for a study to examine historical coffee and input prices in order to arrive at a sustainable minimum price to be implemented by the sector (Perfect Daily Grind 2017). These signs point toward the continued contestation of certiﬁcations as a legitimate avenue of sustainability governance in the coffee sector, as well as critical and continuous cost–beneﬁt analyses by both coffee producers and buyers, even as the industry stands poised to lead the mainstreaming of certiﬁcation. In the coffee sec- tor, this applies in particular to the community of coffee roasters, who create demand for sustainably certiﬁed cof- fee through their sourcing strategies and could pass on marginal price increases to their consumer base, especially if they acted in a concerted fashion (Ponte 2002; Kaplinsky & Fitter 2004; Grabs 2017). This process partially rests on the implicit need for a predominantly stable commercial community in which to advance such ideas and engage in continuous dialogue.
